source_text: sources/comments/files/20251121-5107__Rewiring-America/Rewiring America Comments to FERC Docket RM26-4-000.txt
overall_summary: Rewiring America supports enabling efficient large load integration through demand-side solutions and distributed energy resources. The organization recommends allowing large loads to incorporate off-site, aggregated DERs in interconnection applications and expedited study processes, arguing these resources can deliver equivalent grid benefits as co-located generation while reducing costs for residential customers.

quotes.text: Rewiring America encourages the Commission to remain mindful of the Federal Power Act's cooperative federalism framework. FERC should set floors, not ceilings, and any rules adopted in this proceeding should avoid preempting or impeding state authority.
quotes.concern: Regulatory design should preserve state authority

quotes.text: enabling the efficient integration of new large loads will be central to the next era of grid growth
quotes.concern: Large load integration is strategically important

quotes.text: We particularly welcome DOE's recognition of load flexibility as core grid infrastructure.

demand-side solutions—whether achieved through large load curtailment, energy efficiency that permanently lowers peak demand, or dispatchable distributed energy resources—belong at the center of energy planning, not at the edges.
quotes.concern: Demand-side should be central to planning

quotes.text: Many utilities, and DOE itself, have recognized that virtual power plants, which aggregate and control a network of distributed assets, have rapidly become among the lowest-cost pathways to new megawatts for the grid.
quotes.concern: Aggregated DERs are cost-competitive for grid services

quotes.text: There is also significant latent capacity and flexibility in existing, readily deployable distributed resources that can be mobilized far faster than new generation or major network upgrades.
quotes.concern: Distributed resources offer speed and availability

quotes.text: Studying large loads together with co-located generation can help identify and mitigate network constraints and protect customers, especially residential customers, from avoidable upgrade costs.
quotes.concern: Co-location study reduces costs for all customers

quotes.text: Off-site distributed energy resources can deliver the same types of energy services—generation, load reduction, or transmission relief—that on-site assets provide.
quotes.concern: Off-site DERs provide equivalent grid services

quotes.text: Large loads should therefore be allowed to incorporate aggregated front-of-the-meter and behind-the-meter DERs into their interconnection applications to the extent those resources can offset the transmission or generation upgrades triggered by the request.
quotes.concern: DER eligibility should extend to off-site aggregations

quotes.text: A long-term commitment to secure and maintain such contributions should be treated comparably to co-located generation for modeling purposes.
quotes.concern: Off-site DERs should be modeled like co-located assets

quotes.text: Expanding eligibility to include appropriately located, non-co-located DERs aligns with the principles established in Order No. 2222, which recognizes that DER aggregations can provide dispatchable, reliability-enhancing services that directly affect transmission system needs.
quotes.concern: Order 2222 precedent supports off-site DER inclusion

quotes.text: FERC should expand its expedited interconnection considerations beyond large load curtailability and co-located generation dispatchability to include appropriately located off-site distributed energy resources that can deliver curtailment or dispatch services under contract.
quotes.concern: Expedited processes should include off-site DERs

quotes.text: what matters is equivalent, verifiable performance in mitigating constraints within the same constrained transmission pocket or local grid area, not whether the asset is physically inside or outside the facility fence line.
quotes.concern: Performance location matters more than asset location

quotes.text: By allowing both durable peak reductions and aggregated distributed flexibility to qualify under Principles 3 and 7, FERC can give large loads more options to meet interconnection requirements quickly, affordably, and reliably.
quotes.concern: Flexible rules deliver speed and affordability

bins.name: Study large loads with generation
bins.description: Studying large loads together with co-located generation helps identify and mitigate network constraints, protecting residential customers from unnecessary upgrade costs.

bins.name: Cost relief through demand-side
bins.description: Distributed resources and demand-side solutions are among the lowest-cost pathways to grid services and can reduce residential customer bills by offsetting interconnection upgrade costs.

bins.name: Flexibility as interconnection tool
bins.description: Large loads should use aggregated distributed energy resources and demand flexibility to meet interconnection requirements. Off-site DERs can deliver equivalent grid benefits as co-located generation and should be evaluated on performance location within the same constrained area, not physical site.

bins.name: Timely interconnection integration
bins.description: Efficient large load integration is central to grid growth. Streamlined study processes that incorporate flexibility mechanisms enable quick, reliable, and affordable interconnections.

bins.name: Demand-side centerpiece
bins.description: Demand-side solutions, including load curtailment, energy efficiency, and distributed resources, should be at the center of energy planning, not peripheral. Load flexibility is core grid infrastructure.

bins.name: Off-site DER eligibility
bins.description: Interconnection rules should allow large loads to incorporate off-site, appropriately located aggregated DERs in applications and expedited processes. This aligns with Order 2222 precedent and allows performance-based evaluation rather than requiring physical co-location.

bins.name: Preserve state authority
bins.description: FERC rules should set regulatory floors, not ceilings, preserving state authority and avoiding preemption of state programs and investments.
